topic: Plan a minimal git implementation around content-addressed objects, an index, and guarded workspace transitions.

items.statement: Blob, tree, and commit IDs depend on canonical serialized bytes.
items.excerpt: If object serialization drifts, hashes drift and identity breaks.

items.statement: Checkout must refuse to overwrite dirty or staged paths unless force is explicit.
items.excerpt: Workspace transitions are guarded by index and worktree safety checks.

proposal.summary: Implement `init`, `hash-object`, `write-tree`, `commit-tree`, `status`, and `checkout` over `.minigit/objects` plus a simple JSON index.
claims: The index is enough to drive status and tree writes.
Checkout can rewrite the workspace from a commit snapshot.
acceptance: A user can create commits and restore prior snapshots.
The implementation stays small enough to reason about.

findings.summary: Checkout needs an explicit dirty-path guard.
findings.rationale: A plan that lets checkout rewrite the workspace without a guard will destroy staged or modified files, which breaks the basic safety law users expect from Git-like tools.
findings.suggested_change: Define checkout as a guarded transition that refuses to overwrite dirty or staged paths unless `--force` is explicit.

findings.summary: The index mutation law is missing.
findings.rationale: The proposal names an index but never says which commands own it or how `add`, remove, and checkout move paths between workspace and index. Without that law, `write-tree` correctness is not derivable.
findings.suggested_change: State the index as the sole mutable boundary object and define all path mutations in terms of index transitions.

findings.summary: Canonical object serialization must be part of the plan.
findings.rationale: Content-addressed storage only works when blob, tree, and commit bytes are serialized deterministically. A plan that omits those byte-level forms leaves object identity underspecified.
findings.suggested_change: Define canonical serializers for blob, tree, and commit objects before implementing write or read paths.

verdict.summary: Accept all findings. Make the index the single mutable boundary, define canonical object serializers, and guard checkout transitions.

revised_proposal.summary: Implement a minimal git around canonical object serializers, an index as the only mutable boundary, and guarded checkout transitions.
claims: Blob, tree, and commit bytes are canonically serialized before hashing.
The index, not the workspace, is the source of truth for `write-tree`.
Checkout is a guarded index-plus-worktree transition.
acceptance: Object identity stays stable across reads and writes.
Workspace transitions refuse unsafe overwrites by default.

decisions.rationale: A Git-like tool without checkout guards is semantically broken.
The index must be defined as the mutable boundary to make later steps derivable.
Content-addressed identity requires explicit canonical serialization.

items.statement: Tree materialization snapshots the index, not arbitrary workspace state.
items.excerpt: The index is the boundary object between mutable workspace edits and immutable tree objects.

proposal.summary: The plan now defines canonical object serializers, an index as the mutable boundary, and guarded checkout transitions.
claims: `hash-object`, `write-tree`, and `commit-tree` are all defined over canonical bytes.
`add` populates the index and `write-tree` snapshots the index.
Checkout is guarded against unsafe workspace overwrites.
acceptance: The object model is stable enough to implement.
Unsafe checkout behavior is prevented by design.

findings.summary: Removal semantics still need one explicit state law.
findings.rationale: The revision explains `add` and guarded checkout, but it still leaves path removal split across workspace and index behavior. Without a single remove law, stale index entries can survive into `write-tree` or checkout can resurrect deleted paths incorrectly.
findings.suggested_change: Define `rm` and checkout deletions as index-first transitions that propagate to the workspace only after guard checks pass.

verdict.summary: Accept the removal finding and collapse add, remove, and checkout into one guarded index-first transition law.

revised_proposal.summary: Implement a minimal git where canonical objects are immutable, the index is the sole mutable boundary, and add/remove/checkout are guarded index-first transitions.
claims: `add`, `rm`, and checkout update the index first, then reconcile the workspace under guard rules.
Object hashes remain stable because serialization is canonical.
acceptance: Deletion behavior is derivable from the same transition law as add and checkout.
The plan can be implemented without hidden state between index and workspace.

decisions.rationale: One transition law is simpler and prevents stale index/workspace divergence.

items.statement: The revised proposal now defines add, remove, and checkout as one guarded index-first transition law.
items.excerpt: The index is updated first; workspace changes are applied only after dirty-path guards pass or force is explicit.

proposal.summary: The plan now reduces to canonical object serializers plus one guarded index-first transition law for add, remove, and checkout.
claims: Immutable object identity is derived from canonical bytes.
All mutable path operations flow through the index.
Workspace updates are a guarded projection of index transitions.
acceptance: No extra mutation path exists outside the index-first law.
The implementation boundary between immutable objects and mutable workspace state is explicit.
